annotation_policy: CMT and Koenigsbergian annotations derived from MIPVU-reviewed metaphorical and uncertain lexical units. Each instance cites at least one MIPVU ID with decision_type in METAPHOR_DECISIONS.

entailments: `gloire` frames military conduct or death as publicly radiant renown attached to soldiers or units
Military action and death are understood through fame as covering, radiance, or honor-bearing accompaniment.
cmt.rival_reading: Glory is not a physical covering or companion, but the French phrasing treats it as something one is covered with or dies with.
cmt.justification: MIPVU review rule '' marked 1 unit(s) as metaphor-related or uncertain in this sentence.

instances.gloss_notes: French `gloire` carries military fame, radiance, public renown, and honor; do not flatten it into generic English glory.

entailments: artillery pieces are named as mouths of fire producing death
battle death and bloodshed are framed as produced, sown, or converted into glory-bearing cost
Artillery is understood through bodily mouth and fire imagery.
cmt.rival_reading: Cannons are not literal mouths; the French military term preserves body/fire imagery.
cmt.justification: MIPVU review rule '' marked 3 unit(s) as metaphor-related or uncertain in this sentence.

instances.gloss_notes: French `bouches a feu` is a conventional term for artillery pieces; body imagery remains visible.

entailments: `victoire` is treated as an agent, possession, or hesitant outcome in battle narrative
Battle outcome is understood through personification and possession.
cmt.rival_reading: Victory is abstract but is described as hesitating, deciding, or belonging to an army.
cmt.justification: MIPVU review rule '' marked 1 unit(s) as metaphor-related or uncertain in this sentence.

instances.gloss_notes: French `victoire` may name an event, a result, or a quasi-agent in personified phrasing.

entailments: battle death and bloodshed are framed as produced, sown, or converted into glory-bearing cost
`gloire` frames military conduct or death as publicly radiant renown attached to soldiers or units
Battlefield violence is understood through production, sowing, and sacrificial blood cost.
cmt.rival_reading: Some death is literal, but the phrasing treats death as an output, seed, or glory-bearing sacrificial cost.
cmt.justification: MIPVU review rule '' marked 2 unit(s) as metaphor-related or uncertain in this sentence.

instances.gloss_notes: French `mort` and `sang` may be literal while still serving sacrifice/glory framing.

entailments: battle death and bloodshed are framed as produced, sown, or converted into glory-bearing cost

cmt.rival_reading: Some death is literal, but the phrasing treats death as an output, seed, or glory-bearing sacrificial cost.
